coredata.dc:title: Bending and eigenfrequency analysis of glass fiber reinforced honeycomb sandwich shell panels containing graphene-decorated with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Feng
Liu, Zhongshuai
Selvaraj, Rajeshkumar
Ahsan, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: The present study employs a finite element (FE) formulation to examine the free vibration and bending analyses of sandwich shells made of a glass fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) composite honeycomb core and graphene decorated with graphene quantum dots (GDGQD) reinforced by GFRP composite face layers. The mechanical characteristics of the GFRP composites with GDGQD reinforcement were evaluated using experimental tests. The FE formulation was developed to compute the vibration and bending responses of the sandwich shells via MATLAB software. The Lagrange method is used for dynamic analysis, whereas the principle of potential energy is used for static analysis. The FE model's validity is compared to numerical data reported in the literature in terms of frequencies and deflection, and the results demonstrate excellent agreement. The impacts of the end conditions, core-to-facesheet thickness ratio, curvature ratios, and weight percent of GDGQD are studied further in relation to the bending and dynamic properties of GFRP sandwich shells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glass fiber
Graphene
Honeycomb
Sandwich shell
Dynamic characteristics
Static analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GFRP sandwich cylindrical shell.
GFRP sandwich shell under different mechanical loadings (a) Uniformly distributed load (UDL) (b) Uniformly varying load (UVL).
Various steps involved in fabrication and experimental analysis of GDGQD-GFRP composites.
Preparation of GDGQD-GFRP composite (a) raw materials (b) dispersion (c) fabrication (d) specimen.
Flexural modulus of GDGQD-reinforced GFRP composites.
SEM images of flexural tested fractured GFRP composites (a) Neat GFRP, (b) GDGQD-GFRP.
Tensile modulus of GDGQD reinforced GFRP composites.
SEM images of tensile fractured GFRP laminates (a) Neat GFRP, (b) GDGQD-GFRP.
Non-dimensional in-plane and shear stresses and deflection of sandwich structure.
Comparison of transverse vibration responses of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell. (a) CCCC (b) CFFF end conditions.
First six mode shapes of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell under CFFF and CCCC end conditions.

In-plane and shear stresses of a GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell with UVL. (a)
σ
xx
(b)
σ

(c)
τ
xy
.
Non-dimensional deflection of the CNT reinforced sandwich structure [00/C/00] under UDL.
The non-dimensional frequencies of the sandwich structures.
Natural frequencies of the sandwich cylindrical shells.
The impact of GDGQD wt.% on the natural frequencies (Hz) of GFRP sandwich shell.
The effect of core-facesheet thickness ratios on the natural frequencies (Hz) of GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells.
The influence of curvature ratios on the natural frequencies of GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells.
The central deflection (w) of the sandwich shells with various wt.% of GDGQD under UDL.
The central deflection (w) of the sandwich shells with various wt.% of GDGQD under UVL.
Maximum in-plane and transverse stresses of a GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells.
The central deflection and stresses of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell for various curvature ratios under UDL.

head.ce:title: Bending and eigenfrequency analysis of glass fiber reinforced honeycomb sandwich shell panels containing graphene-decorated with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Merchants Chongqing Communications Technology Research & Design Institute Co., Ltd, Chongqing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Merchants Chongqing Communications Technology Research & Design Institute Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Merchants Chongqing Communications Technology Research & Design Institute Co., Ltd, Chongqing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering Department, National Coalition of Independent Scholars, Vellore, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mechanical Engineering Department, National Coalition of Independent Scholars, Vellore, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Measurements and Control Systems, Silesian University of Technology, Gliwice, 44-100, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Measurements and Control Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Measurements and Control Systems, Silesian University of Technology, Gliwice, 44-100, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study employs a finite element (FE) formulation to examine the free vibration and bending analyses of sandwich shells made of a glass fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) composite honeycomb core and graphene decorated with graphene quantum dots (GDGQD) reinforced by GFRP composite face layers. The mechanical characteristics of the GFRP composites with GDGQD reinforcement were evaluated using experimental tests. The FE formulation was developed to compute the vibration and bending responses of the sandwich shells via MATLAB software. The Lagrange method is used for dynamic analysis, whereas the principle of potential energy is used for static analysis. The FE model's validity is compared to numerical data reported in the literature in terms of frequencies and deflection, and the results demonstrate excellent agreement. The impacts of the end conditions, core-to-facesheet thickness ratio, curvature ratios, and weight percent of GDGQD are studied further in relation to the bending and dynamic properties of GFRP sandwich shells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sandwich composites are frequently used in the aerospace, military, automotive, and marine sectors due to their exceptional strength-to-weight, high corrosion resistance, bearing capacity, and vibration qualities [1,2]. Now a days, the researchers concentrate on nanoparticles for better structural characteristics of the composite sandwich structures in order to increase the stiffness and strength of the sandwich structures made of composite materials [3]. Graphene is a two-dimensional material with high strength and stiffness, and its incorporation into composites has the potential to revolutionize the industry. Therefore, laminated composites have been combined with nanoparticles like carbon nanotubes (CNT) [4] and graphene [5] to improve the mechanical properies of such structures. Ma et al. [6] presented various mechanical dispersion techniques, such as ultrasonication, calandaring, stir, and extrusion procedures, to get around the CNTs' attraction forces and break the bundles of CNTs apart into individual particles. The influence of nanotube content on the elastic modulus of CNT reinforced polymer nanocomposites was discovered experimentally by Andrews et al. [7]. It was shown that adding 10 wt% of CNT to polystyrene increased the Young's modulus of polystyrene nanocomposite films by 178%. The addition of CNTs has shown significant improvements in the strength, stiffness, and toughness of laminated composites [8]. According to Thostenson and Chou [9], the inclusion of 5 wt% of CNTs increased the elastic modulus of the composites by 10% in the randomly oriented version and by 49% in the aligned version. Omidi et al. [10] manufactured an epoxy nanocomposite reinforced with multi-walled CNTs in order to test its mechanical characteristics. The Young's moduli for epoxy composites reinforced with various weight percentages of CNT were assessed. With the addition of more CNTs, it was discovered that the polymer nanocomposite's elastic properties significantly improved.

The effect of graphene oxide (GO) dispersion state on the mechanical characteristics of graphene/epoxy composites was studied by Tang et al [11]. In comparison to composites with poorly dispersed reduced GO, those with highly dispersed reduced GO displayed higher glass transition temperatures and strength. The wrinkled structure of the graphene increased interfacial bonding, which increased the flexural and tensile strength of the graphene/epoxy composites [12]. The π-π interaction increased the mechanical properties of graphene when Duan et al. [13] treated it with benzyl glycidyl ether reinforced with epoxy. Adak et al. [14] achieved consistent graphene reduction and decreased the hazardous character of the chemicals when graphene was thermally reduced. This thermally reduced GO improved inter-laminar adhesion and reduced stress concentration in a carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) composite. Pathak et al. [15] found that 0.3 wt% GO reinforced CFRP composites improved flexural strength, modulus, and interlaminar shear strength by 66%, 72%, and 25%, respectively. According to Wang et al. [16], the 5 wt% graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) reinforced with glass fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) composites increased flexural modulus by 26.3%. Adak et al. [17] obtained an enhancement in the tensile and flexural modulus by 20 and 31%, respectively, with 0.2 wt% GO reinforced with CFRP composite.

Wang and Shen [18] investigated the dynamic properties of a CNT-sandwich structure resting on an elastic foundation. It was found that the dynamic properties of the CNT-sandwich structure were significantly influenced by the CNT volume fraction, temperature, and core to thickness ratio. Zhu et al. [19] numerically conducted a dynamic analysis of thick composite plates reinforced with single walled CNT. With the addition of 6% CNT reinforcement, the central deflection of the CNT reinforced nanocomposite was reduced by 30%. It was discovered that the dynamic properties varied linearly with the volume fraction and the type of CNT distribution. Yas et al. [20] found that adding GNPs to a functionally graded (FG) porous nanocomposite enhanced stiffness because the GNP has a negative thermal expansion coefficient, resulting in superior thermal vibration behavior for the nanocomposite. Safaei et al. [21] found that with an increase in thermal loads and ambient temperature, the frequency of FG CNT-reinforced sandwich plates decreased. First-order shear deformation theory (FSDT) and the artificial spring technique were used by Qin et al. [22] to analyze the dynamic characteristics of FG shells reinforced by graphene platelets (GPL). Bidgoliet al. [23] reported that with an increase in thickness and mass, stiffness enhanced and consequently decreased the natural frequencies of doubly curved sandwich shells. Nguyen et al. [24] enhanced the structural stiffness of an ultralight sandwich plate with a honeycomb core layer reinforced by GNPs. Quoc-Hoa Pham et al. [25] investigated the moisture-driven high-temperature atmosphere dynamic behavior of FG sandwich curved shells with homogenous ceramic cores and varying top and bottom layer thicknesses where the porosity coefficient and stiffness affect composite behavior. According to Garg et al. [26], the end conditions and pore distribution have a significant impact on the beam's free vibration behavior when it comes to sandwich beams with an open-cell metal foam core.

Nguyen et al. [27] developed a computational method to investigate the buckling, vibration, and instability characteristics of sandwich plates with skin layers made of GNP nanocomposites and an auxetic honeycomb core. Swetha et al. [28] used third-order shear deformation theory to study the free and forced vibration properties of a sandwich structure with FG CNT-reinforced composite face sheets and a hybrid honeycomb core. They came to the conclusion that FG-V face sheets with a reduced honeycomb cell size and a greater cell wall thickness display better vibration characteristics. For the bending and characteristics of laminated composite and sandwich spherical shells, Sayyad et al. [29] developed a higher-order closed-form solution utilizing a generalized higher-order shell theory. Using the FSDT, Majidi-Mozafari et al. [30] looked at the dynamic properties of a sandwich plate reinforced with GPLs and encased in piezoelectric layers. The findings demonstrate that a minor addition of GPLs as reinforcement nanofillers greatly increases the rigidity of the plate. The free and forced vibration of a sandwich plate reinforced with GNP was examined by Mohseni and Shakouri [31]. According to the findings, introducing a modest number of GNP can dramatically increase the rigidity of the plate. A modified version of the Biot model was used by Pan et al. [32] to explore the damping behavior of nanocomposites reinforced with CNT. In comparison to pure epoxy, they found that the nanocomposite with 0.4 wt% CNT reinforcement improves the damping ratio by 41%. The damping characteristics of nature-inspired sandwich composites containing reduced GO were studied by Feng et al. [33]. Li et al. [34] used experimental and theoretical techniques to explore the thermal-vibration ageing behaviors of laminated cylindrical shells coated with polyurea. In order to predict the nonlinear vibration behaviors of fiber metal plates with multiple viscoelastic layers at various excitation levels, Li et al. [35] suggested an analytical model. The optimal layout of vibro-impact resistant laminated plates with polyurea coating was studied by Li et al. [36] using FSDT. Maneengam et al. [37] characterized the vibration and damping properties of CNT reinforced sandwich shells using higher order shear deformation theory (HSDT). They found that increasing the CNT content in sandwich shells enhanced the vibration and damping properties.

The literature review contains the majority of research on the vibration behavior of composite structures without fiber reinforcement that are reinforced with CNT and graphene. However, the dynamic and bending properties of graphene decorated with graphene quantum dots (GDGQD) reinforced GFRP composite sandwich shells with GFRP honeycomb cores have not been studied. Initially, the GDGQD reinforced GFRP composites are fabricated and tested to ascertain their mechanical characteristics through experiments. Following that, a finite element (FE) model built on HSDT was developed and validated with numerical findings in the literature, and it exhibits an incredibly high level of agreement for sandwich structures. The dynamic and bending properties of GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells were also studied parametrically with respect to GDGQD weight percent, curvature ratio, and thickness ratios.

In this work, the FE model was developed using a three-layered sandwich shell made of GFRP face sheets and a GFRP honeycomb core. A sandwich shell is schematically represented in Fig. 1 . The detailed mechanical loading information is shown in Fig. 2 . The sandwich shell's length is L, the face sheet thickness is hs , the core thickness is hhc , and the radius of curvature is R, respectively. The constitutive equations for the top and base layers are developed using higher order displacement field components, as shown in [37].

The governing equation for the dynamic analysis of the sandwich shell is obtained using the Lagrange's equation and is represented by
(19)

where d is the element displacement field vector and k, m, f are the element stiffness, mass, and force vectors. Using MATLAB® coding, the mass, stiffness, and force matrices are integrated for all the elements to get the system governing equation, which is shown as
(20)

where M, K, F are the global mass, stiffness, force vector, and q is the global displacement vector.

The concept of minimal potential energy was used to derive the equation of equilibrium for the static analysis of sandwich shells, which is represented as
(21)
δ (
V T
- W ) =
0

where W, VT and
δ
are the work done, total strain energy and variation symbol.

Fig. 3 shows the various steps followed in fabrication and experimental analysis of GDGQD reinforced GRP composites. Pristine graphite flakes, phosphoric acid, hydrochloric acid, hydrogen peroxide, concentrated sulphuric acid, ethanol, N-N dimethylformamide, potassium permanganate, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA), N-N-bis (2-aminoethyl) ethane-1, 2-diamine, and glass fiber are the materials used for the fabrication process. A flowchart of the fabrication process is shown in Fig. 4 . The fabrication process was conducted based on references [38,39]. The modified Hummers technique was used to make graphene oxide (GO), and solvothermal reduction of GO was used to make GDGQDs. The ethanol and the GDGQD nanofiller were combined and then sonicated for 30 min. The epoxy was then added to the mixture and sonicated for an additional hour in a cold bath. On a magnetic stirrer with a heated plate, the solution was swirled at 250 rpm for an hour at 50 °C to allow the ethanol to completely evaporate. After the mixture had been vacuum-degassed, a 1:10 ratio of curing agent was added. Additionally, a hand layup method was employed to completely fill a twelve-layer glass fibre composite with the epoxy/graphene matrix. The fabricated composites were then dried in an industrial oven.

The flexural properties of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP composites were tested according to ASTM D-790. The flexural modulus values for the GFRP laminates are shown in Fig. 5 . The difference in the reinforcing behavior of glass fiber was conveyed from the flexural modulus values. The flexural modulus was enhanced due to the presence of fillers in the epoxy-fiber interface. The GFRP composites with 0.75 wt% GDGQD reinforcement show a 29.60% improvement in flexural modulus. The higher improvement in modulus for GFRP was due to the incredible rigidity offered by the filler, which enhanced the load bearing ability. Stronger interfacial interaction was favored by efficient dispersion and the higher aspect ratio of GDGQD. During the flexural test, the bottom portion of the specimen was under tension, while the top section was compressed. Fig. 6 (a) depicts neat epoxy-fibers on the surface as well as longer, projecting fibers that are free of the matrix and are visible as a result of inadequate interfacial adhesion. Fig. 6 (b) denotes fracture and delamination due to filler matrix interaction. According to these perspectives, the GDGQD dispersion should have strengthened the GFRP composites.

The tensile properties of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP composites were tested according to ASTM D3039. For each composite composition, five samples were tested. Fig. 7 displays the average modulus values. The tensile modulus of GDGQD reinforced GFRP increased over the neat material by 32.50%. The well-distributed and non-agglomerated nanofillers that improved interfacial bonding with the laminate surfaces were the cause of the increased tensile modulus. The binding force of filler in epoxy on to GFRP obviously affects the interface strength of the GDGQD/GFRP composite material. The modulus of the GFRP laminate significantly improved with the content of filler, which was due to the enhanced stress transfer across the matrix. Thus, here, a strong interaction between GDGQD/epoxy and GFRP persists through the functional group interaction that enhances the stiffness of the composites. Fig. 8 (a) shows neat GFRP with more debonded fibers. The matrix traces (Fig. 8b) were found on the surfaces of fibers and revealed an interfacial interaction between the fibers and matrix. These studies show matrix toughening and enhanced fiber–matrix interfaces with GDGQD reinforcement, where the improved laminate stiffness was caused by functional group interactions at the nanoscale.

The results in terms of deflections and natural frequencies are compared with the findings of existing literature to demonstrate the effectiveness of the mathematical model that was developed. MATLAB® 2021a software was used to run the numerical simulations on a laptop powered by an Intel® Core i5-8250U@1.60 GHz CPU.This section simulates the sandwich structures under different end conditions, and the material and geometric properties are taken from references. In this validation investigation, the numerical solutions for a sandwich structure provided by Singh and Sahoo [40] are compared. The results are displayed in Table 1 . The Singh and Sahoo [40] results and those obtained using the current FEM exhibit a notable degree of consistency.

The numerical data from Sahoo and Singh [41] and Chalak et al. [42] were compared to the current FEM, which is shown in Fig. 9 . The geometrical and material details of the sandwich structure are: E1/E2 = 25, G12 = 0.5E2, G23 = 0.2E2, ν12 = 0.25, L/h = 100, core thickness = 0.8 h, face sheet thickness = 0.1 h, total thickness = h, E1c/E2 = 0.04, G12/E2 = 0.016 and G23/E2 = 0.06. Fig. 9 compares the sandwich structure's non-dimensional stresses and deflections computed using the present FEM with those available in the literature. They are confirmed to be in excellent agreement. The derived FE model is further examined for clamped sandwich structures, with material properties taken from reference [43]. The results (Table 2 ) of the current FE model and those presented in literature have excellent agreement.

The FEM's reliability is further demonstrated by comparing the results obtained with the presen FEM to those reported in the literature. The material and geometrical details of the CFRP sandwich shell are: E1 = 119 GPa, E2 = 8.7 GPa, ν12 = 0.32, G12 = 4 GPa, G23 = 3 GPa, ρ = 1560 kg/m3, L = 0.3 m, B = 0.3 m, R/L = 25, face sheet thickness = 0.625 mm, core thickness = 2.5 mm, ply orientation = [0°/90°/0°/90°/0°], core shear modulus Gxz = 182.29 MPa, Gyz = 166.16 MPa and ρ = 333.33 kg/m3 . The outcomes are displayed in Table 3 . Excellent agreement can be seen between the results produced by the current FEM and those of Maneengam et al. [37].

The static and dynamic properties of GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells was studied using the FE approach. The following parameters were used to evaluate the static and dynamic properties of the GFRP sandwich shell that was subjected to UDL and UVL: weight percentage of GDGQD, end conditions, core-facesheet thickness ratio, and curvature ratio. The sandwich shell under consideration has the following geometric dimensions: length (L) = 600 mm, breadth (B) = 600 mm, radius of curvature (R) = 1200 mm, core thickness = 5 mm and ply thickness = 0.208 mm. The material properties for the GFRP honeycomb core are taken from our previous work [38] such as: shear modulus Gxz = 182.29 MPa, Gyz = 166.16 MPa and density ρ = 333.3 kg/m3 . Section 3 contains the material parameters that were considered for the analysis, such as: E1 = E2 = -1.12Gr2 + 3.62Gr + 6.8 GPa, ν12 = -0.008Gr2 − 0.068Gr + 0.29, G12 = -0.4Gr2 + 1.40Gr + 2.63 GPa, and ρ = 24.8Gr2 + 13.4Gr + 1718.10 kg/m3, where Gr is the wt.% of GDGQD.

The effects of increasing the weight percentage of GDGQD (0, 0.25, 0.5 and 0.75) on the dynamic properties of the GFRP sandwich shell under various end conditions are examined, including simply supported-simply supported-simply supported-simply supported (SSSS), clamped–clamped–clamped–clamped (CCCC), simply supported-free-simply supported-free (SFSF), clamped-free-clamped-free (CFCF), and clamped-free-free-free (CFFF). The first six mode shapes of the GFRP sandwich shell are shown in Fig. 10 . Table 4 demonstrates that when GFRP sandwich shells with GDGQD reinforcement were compared to neat GFRP sandwich shells, the natural frequencies were greater. The fundamental frequency of the GFRP sandwich shell reinforced with GDGQD is superior by 14.09% and 14.81%. under CCCC and CFFF conditions. This is due to the significant interfacial interaction between GDGQD and glass fibre, which contributes to the GFRP sandwich shell's increased rigidity. A similar tendency is also seen in Liu et al. [44] for FG-graphene platelets reinforced composite shells. Fig. 11 show the dynamic responses of GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell. It can be shown that when compared to neat GFRP sandwich shells, GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells have a smaller peak response magnitude for all modes. Additionally, it can be seen that the GFRP sandwich shells' lowest and greatest natural frequencies are produced by the CFFF and CCCC end conditions.

Table 5 presents the outcomes of a study on the effects of changing the core-facesheet thickness ratios on the vibration properties of the GFRP sandwich shell under CCCC end conditions. As the ratio of the core-to-facesheet thickness rises, it has been found that the natural frequency of the GFRP sandwich shell increases. This could be because increasing the thickness ratio increases the shell's stiffness. Table 6 displays the results of an investigation into the effects of increasing curvature ratios under CFCF end circumstances on the dynamic properties of the GFRP sandwich shell. The results show that for all the modes examined, the natural frequency drops as the curvature ratio increases. This may be because flat structures absorb less bending and stretching energy than sandwich shells with the least amount of curvature, which results in higher natural frequencies.

The influence of GDGQD weight percent on the static behavior of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell under various end conditions, including two types of mechanical loading situations (UDL and UVL), is analyzed and is illustrated in Tables 7 and 8
. The neat GFRP sandwich shell deflections, which have no relation to the inclusion of GDGQD, are also displayed for comparison's sake. As anticipated, the center deflection significantly decreases after GDGQD is added to the sandwich shells, and a consistent deflection reduction is shown as the weight percentage of GDGQD grows. The deflection of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell is 27.42% smaller than that of the neat GFRP sandwich shell under CCCC end conditions. It's also important to note that the central deflection decreases as the weight percentage of GDGQD grows and that it is higher under the UDL than under the UVL. This is due to the improved rigidity of the GFRP sandwich shells with GDGQD reinforcement. It can also be seen that as the ratio of the core to face thickness increases, the central deflections decrease. The present results reflect the anticipated trend because the deflection of a stiff structure diminishes as core thickness increases. Based on the findings, it can be seen that the GDGQD reinforcement seems to lessen the bending deflection of sandwich shells more efficiently.

The effects of the ratio between the thicknesses of the core and factsheets on GDGQD-reinforced GFRP sandwich shells under various end circumstances have been investigated; the findings (normal (σxx), (σyy) and shear stresses (τxy)) are shown in Table 9 . Normal and transverse stress decrease under various end conditions with the influence of the thickness ratio. For all investigated end conditions, it manifests itself as a decrease in stress as the core-facesheet thickness ratio increases. As a result, it can be inferred that the stresses are less for thick GFRP sandwich shells as compared to thin GFRP sandwich shells. It was also observed that the magnitude of in-plane and transverse stresses developed in SSSS end conditions is the maximum in comparison to all other end conditions. This is attributed to the in-plane stiffness variation offered by the end condition. The examination of the impacts of the curvature ratio on the deflection and stresses of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell under the CFCF end conditions is shown in Table 10 and Fig. 12 . The curvature ratio has a substantial impact on the center deflection, inplane and transverse stresses of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell. As the curvature ratio increases, it manifests as an increase in deflection as well as inplane and transverse stresses at all measured end conditions. This might be due to the fact that flat structures provide less bending and stretching energy than sandwich shells with the least amount of curvature, which are more rigid.

The finite element approach is used in this study to analyze the static and dynamic behavior of GFRP sandwich shells reinforced with GDGQD. The experimental test was performed on fabricated composites, and it was observed that the tensile modulus and flexural modulus increased by 32.50% and 29.60%, for GDGQD reinforced GFRP composites compared with neat GFRP composites. The mechanical properties of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP composite were enhanced by the interfacial interaction of GDGQD with the fiber periphery through functionality. The GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells exhibit higher natural frequencies compared to GFRP sandwich shells without GDGQD reinforcement. The natural frequencies of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shell is 14.81% greater than those of the neat GFRP sandwich shells under CFFF end conditions. It was observed that as the weight percent of GDGQD increased, the natural frequencies also increased. The natural frequencies of the GDGQD reinforced GFRP sandwich shells are considerably influenced by the core-facesheet thickness ratio, curvature ratio, and end conditions. In addition, the bending responses of the GFRP sandwich shells were examined, and it was revealed that GDGQD-reinforced GFRP sandwich shells had lower center deflections than neat GFRP sandwich shells regardless of core-facesheet thickness ratio, end conditions, and curvature ratio. Finally, it can be noticed that the vibration and bending behavior of GFRP sandwich shells are significantly influenced by the bonding between the glass fibers and the GDGQD/polymer matrix. Therefore, the current research may be used as a reference for the effective design and development of GDGQD-reinforced CFRP composites for engineering applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
